title: Social Control
examTip: Examiners distinguish between formal and informal social control — always give examples of both types and explain which agencies carry out each. Foucault's Panopticon is a high-value conceptual example for surveillance questions.
introTips: Formal control = official agencies with legal power (police, courts, prison).
Informal control = everyday social pressure without legal authority (family, peer group, media).
Foucault (1977) — the Panopticon: surveillance makes people self-discipline even when not watched.
intro: Most people don't commit serious crimes — not primarily because they fear the police, but because they've internalised the belief that harming others is wrong. That internalisation is the work of social control: the vast web of pressures, rewards, punishments, and expectations that shape behaviour from the family dinner table to the prison cell. Understanding how society maintains order tells us as much about power as it does about conformity.

keyPoints.heading: What Is Social Control?
keyPoints.content: Social control refers to all the ways in which society encourages or pressures individuals to conform to its norms and rules. Without social control, Thomas Hobbes argued life would be 'nasty, brutish, and short' — a war of all against all. There are two broad categories. Formal social control: official agencies with legal authority and the power to enforce rules through sanctions — the police, courts, prison service, probation service, social services, and immigration control. Informal social control: everyday social pressures that operate without legal authority — the family's expectations, peer group pressure, the fear of gossip, religious moral codes, and professional norms. Max Weber identified the state as having the monopoly on legitimate violence — only the state can legally use physical force to enforce compliance. Travis Hirschi's control theory (1969) — 'Causes of Delinquency': people conform when they have strong 'social bonds' to conventional society. Four bonds: attachment (strong relationships with family, school, friends), commitment (investment in conventional future goals — a university place to lose), involvement (time occupied by conventional activities — sport, work — leaving no time for crime), and belief (acceptance of the moral legitimacy of society's rules). Crime results when these bonds are weakened or broken.
writeBullets: Social control — all ways society pressures individuals to conform to norms
Formal social control — official agencies with legal power (police, courts, prison)
Informal social control — everyday social pressures without legal authority
Weber — state has the monopoly on legitimate violence
Hirschi (1969) control theory — strong social bonds prevent crime
Four bonds: attachment, commitment, involvement, belief
keyPoints.memTip: ACIB — Attachment, Commitment, Involvement, Belief. Hirschi's four bonds that keep us conforming.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Hirschi is often overlooked but frequently appears in exam questions on why people conform rather than why they deviate.

keyPoints.heading: Formal Social Control: Agencies
keyPoints.content: The police are the most visible formal control agency: enforce laws, detect and arrest offenders, maintain public order, and have powers of stop and search (Section 60 of the Criminal Justice and Public Order Act 1994 — doesn't even require reasonable suspicion). Courts: magistrates' courts (minor offences) and crown courts (serious offences) — determine guilt and impose sentences (fines, community orders, custodial sentences). Prison: the ultimate formal sanction — 81,000+ people incarcerated in England and Wales (2024). Purposes of punishment: retribution (punishment for its own sake), deterrence (individual and general), rehabilitation (reform the offender — 48% of prisoners reoffend within 12 months, Ministry of Justice — suggesting prison fails at rehabilitation), and incapacitation (keeping dangerous people away from society). Probation service: supervises offenders in the community — community orders, electronic tagging, unpaid work requirements. Social services: protect vulnerable people, especially children (Children Act 1989 — local authorities have a statutory duty to safeguard children at risk of harm). The criminal justice system has been extensively criticised for class and racial bias — Marxists argue it serves ruling-class interests; conflict theorists argue it criminalises the powerless.
writeBullets: Police — enforce laws, arrest offenders, maintain order, stop and search powers
Courts — determine guilt; impose sentences (fines, community orders, custodial)
Prison — 81,000+ incarcerated in England and Wales; 48% reoffend within 12 months
Four purposes of punishment: retribution, deterrence, rehabilitation, incapacitation
Probation service — supervises offenders in the community
Criminal justice system criticised for class and racial bias by Marxists and conflict theorists
keyPoints.memTip: RDIR — Retribution, Deterrence, Incapacitation, Rehabilitation. Four purposes of punishment.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The 48% reoffending rate is a powerful statistic that challenges the rehabilitation purpose of prison — worth memorising.

keyPoints.heading: Informal Social Control: Agencies
keyPoints.content: Informal social control is exercised by everyday institutions and relationships — and is often more powerful than formal control because it shapes our fundamental values, not just our fear of punishment. Family: the primary and most powerful agent of socialisation and social control — teaches children right from wrong, instils shame and guilt for deviant behaviour, and rewards conformity with love and approval. A child who internalises the belief that stealing is wrong won't steal even when no one is watching — that is the most effective social control of all. Religion: provides a moral framework (the Ten Commandments, the Five Pillars), community pressure to conform, and the threat of divine punishment. Secularisation (declining religious belief and practice) may reduce this control. Education: teaches norms and values (the hidden curriculum — punctuality, respect for authority, competition), uses formal rewards (grades, praise) and punishments (detention, exclusion). Peer groups: especially powerful for teenagers — social acceptance, fear of ostracism, and pressure to conform to peer norms shape behaviour significantly. Media: shapes perceptions of normality and deviance — moral panics stigmatise deviant groups (Stan Cohen, 1972). Workplace: professional codes of conduct, HR sanctions, reputation. Community: neighbourhood watch schemes, local gossip, ostracism for deviance.
writeBullets: Family — most powerful agent; internalises values; rewards conformity with love and approval
Religion — moral framework; community pressure; threat of divine punishment
Education — hidden curriculum; formal rewards and punishments
Peer groups — especially powerful for teenagers; fear of ostracism shapes behaviour
Media — shapes perceptions of normality; moral panics stigmatise deviant groups (Stan Cohen 1972)
Informal control often more effective than formal — internalised values prevent crime, not just fear of police
keyPoints.memTip: FREPM — Family, Religion, Education, Peers, Media. Five key informal control agencies.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The argument that informal control (internalised values) is more effective than formal control (punishment) is a strong evaluation point.

keyPoints.heading: Surveillance and Social Control
keyPoints.content: Michel Foucault (1977) — 'Discipline and Punish': traced a historical shift in punishment from public spectacle (execution, torture as theatrical performance — designed to demonstrate the sovereign's power) to invisible surveillance and self-discipline. His central metaphor is Jeremy Bentham's Panopticon: a prison design in which inmates are arranged in cells around a central watchtower. Prisoners can never see whether a guard is actually watching — but they know they might be. This uncertainty means they behave as if they are always being observed. Foucault argues that modern society operates on the same principle: the internalisation of surveillance produces self-discipline. Modern surveillance: the UK has an estimated 5.9 million CCTV cameras — more per person than almost any other country in the world. Internet surveillance (GCHQ bulk data collection revealed by Edward Snowden in 2013, social media monitoring). Workplace monitoring (keystroke logging, email surveillance, productivity tracking). Gary Marx (1988) — 'Undercover': covert policing and undercover surveillance erodes civil liberties. Predictive policing algorithms — software that predicts where crime is likely to occur and who is likely to offend — have been shown to perpetuate racial bias (by reflecting the racially biased arrest patterns in the historical data they are trained on), raising fundamental questions about surveillance and justice.
writeBullets: Foucault (1977) 'Discipline and Punish' — shift from public punishment to invisible surveillance
Panopticon (Bentham) — prisoners behave as if always watched; internalise surveillance
UK has ~5.9 million CCTV cameras — more per person than almost anywhere
Internet surveillance — GCHQ bulk data collection (Snowden 2013), social media monitoring
Predictive policing algorithms — perpetuate racial bias by learning from biased historical data
Gary Marx (1988) 'Undercover' — covert policing erodes civil liberties
keyPoints.memTip: Panopticon = you don't need to be watched to feel watched. That's the modern surveillance state.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Foucault's Panopticon is one of the most sophisticated concepts at GCSE level — if a student can explain and apply it, it signals real sociological thinking.

0.question: Hirschi's (1969) control theory argues that people conform because of what?
options: Strong social bonds to conventional society (attachment, commitment, involvement, belief)
Fear of formal punishment from police, courts, and prison
Biological drives that make conformity the natural human state
The internalisation of Marxist ideology spread by the ruling class
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Hirschi argued that it is not the threat of punishment that keeps most people law-abiding — it is their social bonds (to family, school, careers, and moral beliefs) that give them something to lose if they offend.
0.wrongFeedback: Hirschi's theory is specifically about why people DON'T commit crime — he argued it's not just fear of being caught. What do people with conventional stakes in society stand to lose?

3.question: Foucault's concept of the Panopticon describes which aspect of modern social control?
options: Surveillance that makes people self-discipline because they might be watched at any time
Public punishment that physically deters crime through visible executions
The criminal justice system's formal power to incarcerate offenders
Peer group pressure that forces conformity through social ostracism
3.correctFeedback: Correct. The Panopticon is a prison design where inmates can never tell if they're being watched — so they behave as if they always are. Foucault used this to explain how modern surveillance (CCTV, internet monitoring) produces self-discipline without needing constant actual observation.
3.wrongFeedback: The Panopticon is Foucault's response to the older model of public spectacle punishment. In the Panopticon model, the power isn't in the punishment itself — it's in what the possibility of being watched does to behaviour.

commonMistakes: Listing only formal control agencies (police, courts, prison) — informal control (family, peers, religion) is equally important and often more effective.
Confusing Hirschi's four bonds — attachment, commitment, involvement, belief — with each other. Practice defining each one separately.
Saying Foucault's Panopticon means everyone is always being watched — the key point is the UNCERTAINTY. People don't know if they're watched, so they behave as if they always are.
Treating rehabilitation as a proven success — the 48% reoffending rate within 12 months is powerful evidence that prison frequently fails at this purpose.
Not distinguishing between the purposes of punishment (retribution, deterrence, rehabilitation, incapacitation) — these are separate goals that can conflict.
examTips: Always distinguish formal from informal social control with specific examples — naming the police, courts, and prison for formal, and family, peers, and religion for informal.
Hirschi's control theory (1969) is an alternative to asking 'why do people commit crime?' — it asks 'why do most people NOT commit crime?' This is a sophisticated framing that earns evaluation marks.
Foucault's Panopticon can be applied to CCTV, workplace monitoring, and social media — use it to show you can apply concepts to contemporary examples.
revisionCardBullets: Social control — all ways society pressures individuals to conform to norms and rules
Formal social control — official agencies with legal power (police, courts, prison, probation)
Informal social control — everyday social pressures (family, religion, peers, media, workplace)
Weber — state has monopoly on legitimate violence (only state can legally use force)
Hirschi (1969) — social bonds: attachment, commitment, involvement, belief prevent crime
Police — stop and search, arrest, community policing; criticised for racial bias
Prison — 81,000+ England and Wales; 48% reoffend within 12 months (fails at rehabilitation)
Purposes of punishment: retribution, deterrence, rehabilitation, incapacitation
Family — most powerful informal agent; internalises values
Stan Cohen (1972) — media creates moral panics; informal stigmatisation of deviant groups
Foucault (1977) 'Discipline and Punish' — Panopticon; surveillance produces self-discipline
UK has ~5.9 million CCTV cameras; predictive policing algorithms perpetuate racial bias
